#Summary1=(human1) The defense lawyer argues that the trial judge wrongly used his discretion when he said there was manifest necessity for a mistrial based on the defense's opening and an alleged lack of consultation, because the judge had already found his curative instruction remedied any jury nullification problem and then mistakenly assumed there was no consultation.
#Text=SJC 13660 .
#Text=Commonwealth versus Elena Gaston .
#Text=Okay , Attorney .
#Text=Whenever you 're ready .
#Text=Good afternoon .
#Text=May it please the court .
#Text=My name is Andrew , and I represent Elena Gaston .
#Text=This case presents an issue that this court has never confronted , and that is whether a potential future claim of ineffective assistance can create a manifest necessity for a mistrial .

#Text=But I do n't think this court actually needs to reach that broader question , because the question presented on these facts is actually much narrower , and that is whether there was a manifest necessity where one , the judge erroneously inferred a complete lack of consultation based on counsel 's statements about the opening .
#Text=Two , where the judge appears to have mistakenly believed that the defendant 's explicit consent is required prior to a concession .
#Text=And three , the defendant neither alleged a lack of consultation --
#Text=So sorry , Defense Attorney argues jury nullification and examines on jury nullification .
#Text=What 's the judge to do ?
#Text=Just let it go forward ?
#Text=Well , in terms of the jury nullification issue , I believe the judge addressed that .
#Text=That was sort of the first potential basis for the mistrial that he considered .
#Text=Um , he gave a forceful curative instruction , and he found repeatedly and explicitly that the jury could and would follow it so there could be no prejudice to the Commonwealth .
#Text=And then the defense attorney just does it again , right ?
#Text=Well , no .

#Text=I think if you when you look closely at the record , you 'll see that even prior to , um , the , the evidentiary portion of the trial beginning , there 's some motions in limine about what kind of questions can be asked .
#Text=And he says " even though force and coercion are not elements , you are allowed to inquire about that " .

column1: #Text=And the judge says there was n't anything surprising about the cross-examination .
#Text=" I expected there to be questions about lack of force , lack of coercion . "
#Text=Um , so I think that where the judge has given that forceful instruction and where he has found repeatedly and explicitly , the jury can and will abide by it , that sort of closes the book .
#Text=But it 's sort of a combination , is n't it ?
#Text=Because it 's it 's not just that the opening argued jury nullification .
#Text=It 's that the opening foreclosed the only defense that had been articulated by defense counsel to the judge , which is lack of knowledge .
#Text=So the the the opening did two things .
#Text=It both removed --
#Text=It basically undermined that defense , the only defense and then also argued jury nullification .
#Text=So do n't we need to look at that in , you know , both of those together when we 're reviewing , um , the judge 's decision to allow the mistrial or grant the mistrial ?
#Text=I disagree because you have the trial judge making that factual finding that the jury can and would follow the curative instruction .
#Text=And under Arizona v Washington , we defer to that trial judge 's assessment because the trial judge observed the jurors during their voir dire .
#Text=They -- he observed the opening as it was given .
#Text=He observed the tone .
#Text=He observed the jurors during the curative instruction .
#Text=And for those reasons , we have to defer a finding .
#Text=Was there a finding ?
#Text=Was there a finding that the judge believed that the instruction would be followed , or was it ?
#Text=Yes .
#Text=Okay .
#Text=Yes he does .
#Text=Um , and I can --
#Text=But I think the judge said that that was based on his assessment at the time he made that ruling .
#Text=And then after that , there were three witnesses called and the subject matter of the cross-examination .
#Text=I 'm sorry , um .
#Text=The subject matter of the cross-examination continued down the same line , which was lack of compulsion , right ?
#Text=It did n't it did n't try to do anything with the maybe a legally relevant -- a legally relevant line of defense .
#Text=Well , again , I would say --
#Text=Well , just as an initial matter , so the judge adheres to his conclusion that the jury could and would follow it .
#Text=So you want us to defer to the judge 's findings , the first finding , and then ignore the judge 's finding later on and say there 's an abuse of discretion ?
#Text=No , I 'm not asking you to ignore the finding .
#Text=I 'm saying the finding about whether the efficacy of the curative instruction is a particular type of finding that we defer to .
#Text=And the judge does say , it 's at page 185 --
#Text=But unrepentant , the lawyer just keeps doing the same thing witness after witness , correct ?

#Text=No , I disagree , and the judge disagreed as well , because when the Commonwealth makes that argument , the judge says , " actually , I was not surprised by anything that happened during cross-examination ; we had this discussion prior to trial that he would be allowed to inquire into that " .
#Text=It 's not an element , but it 's a proper basis for inquiry .
#Text=Um , so I think that the judge has addressed that situation and you have to defer to his finding on that , and he does continue to adhere to it on Monday , the day of the mistrial .
#Text=So if you look at the transcripts , page 12 and 14 , he says " I continue to believe the jury would follow this instruction " .
#Text=And it 's not until the Commonwealth raises this issue of lack of consultation that I am now reconsidering .
#Text=I came into court today ready to reaffirm my denial of the mistrial , and then the Commonwealth raised this issue for the first time .
#Text=And on the basis of that , I find there 's manifest necessity .
#Text=The problem with that finding is that it 's predicated on this erroneous factual finding that there is a total lack of consultation , right ?
#Text=What trial counsel says is I did n't talk to her about my opening specifically .
#Text=There 's no other information .
#Text=The judge asks him once .
#Text=He says " no , I did n't speak to her about my opening " .
#Text=The judge later says " well , did n't you just tell me you did n't talk to her about your opening ? "
#Text=And he says " yes , that 's correct " .
#Text=Those are the only two questions we have .
